source: Labour unrest in Malaya May Day demonstrations. Includes 6 photographs depicting May Day demonstrations, Singapore police and crowd engage at Syed Road Bridge. Dated 1940__CO273:662:10
filename: Labour unrest in Malaya May Day demonstrations. Includes 6 photographs depicting May Day demonstrations, Singapore police and crowd engage at Syed Road Bridge. Dated 1940__CO273:662:10.txt
summary:
- **Core topic/purpose:** Colonial Office/Straits Settlements correspondence and intelligence on **labour unrest in Malaya (especially Singapore)**, linking strikes and May Day disturbances to **communist organisation and propaganda**, and considering policy/legislative responses.  
- **Geography & period:** Primarily **Singapore and Malaya** during the **early WWII period (late 1939–1940)**; key events cluster around **May–August 1940**.  
- **Major events (strikes):** Reports note **over 130 strikes in Singapore** after the outbreak of war, initially tied to **cost-of-living increases** and later described as increasingly **political**; major disputes included the **Singapore Harbour Board/dockyard** and **rubber factory/packing industry strikes** affecting rubber shipments.  
- **May Day clash (1 May 1940):** An **illegal communist-organised demonstration** led to a violent confrontation at/near **Syed Alwi Road Bridge / Rochore Canal Road**; a police picket (reported as **20 men**) was attacked by a crowd described as **~400 armed with sticks/bottles**; police fired revolvers in self-defence.  
- **Casualties & inquest findings:** Telegrams and inquest evidence indicate **two Chinese deaths** (Neo Koh Kiat—died **1 May 1940**; Loh Neo Choh—died **2 May 1940**) from gunshot wounds; the **Coroner’s Court (8 May 1940, Coroner W. MacQuarrie)** jury returned verdicts of **“accidental death caused by justifiable shooting by the Police in the execution of their duty,”** with a rider commending **Inspector P.S. Gordon** and **L/Cpl. 1328**.  
- **Key actors/organisations:** **Sir Shenton Thomas** (Governor/High Commissioner), the **Officer Administering the Government (Straits Settlements)**, **Singapore Police** (Inspector Gordon; Inspector-General A.H. Dickinson), **Colonial Office/Secretary of State** (incl. Lord Lloyd), **Mr. Creech Jones, MP**, employer bodies (e.g., **Singapore Chamber of Commerce**, **United Planting Association of Malaya**, mine owners), and groups described as communist fronts (e.g., **Singapore All-Trades (Red) General Labour Union**, **Anti-Enemy Backing-Up Society**).  
- **Decisions/proposals/policy responses:** Emphasis on meeting “reasonable” wage demands via **cost-of-living allowances**, maintaining essential services under **Defence Regulations**, **arrests/banishment/deportation of alien communist agitators** (e.g., Firestone strike: **37 men and 9 women arrested** on **22 June 1940**; later reference to **229 alien communist agitators** arrested for banishment), and advancing **Trade Unions/Industrial Courts legislation** while trying to prevent communist capture of unions.  
- **Notable numbers/dates:** **1 May 1940** riot; **8 May 1940** inquests; **May 4 1940** Firestone strike start (factory said to employ **200 men, 100 women**); **4,285 tons** of rubber shipped to the UK in the **first week of June 1940**; **15,500 tons** shipping space requested; intelligence note of **$500** spent on communist printing apparatus; discussion of a proposed **$15,000,000** scale cost for anglicising Chinese education (raised as contentious).
